reasoning: Friedrich Merz was elected Chancellor on May 6, 2025, following a snap election. By early November 2025, he remains in office, heading a grand coalition with the SPD. Dislodging a German Chancellor is structurally difficult (requiring a constructive vote of no confidence to elect a successor), making sudden removal almost impossible unless a new unwieldy majority forms (unlikely due to the 'firewall' against the AfD). Further, if the coalition were to collapse and fresh elections triggered, Merz would likely remain acting Chancellor through the end of 2025. While he faces low approval ratings and some intra-coalition friction, there are zero indications he intends to resign voluntarily after spending decades striving for the chancellorship. With less than two months remaining in the year, the likelihood of him suddenly resigning or being removed is limited almost entirely to unexpected health emergencies or unprecedented massive scandals.
